methods.method_name: a-cellulose extraction and oxygen isotopic measurement
methods.description: This method describes the process for selecting, preparing, and analyzing tree-ring samples to determine oxygen isotopic ratios (d18O) in earlywood and latewood a-cellulose. It involves physical separation, chemical extraction, homogenization, drying, and mass spectrometry.

steps.description: Nine tree-ring cores (each one from different trees) with homogeneous growth patterns and no evident growth aberrations were selected for isotope analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: select tree cores based on growth patterns and lack of aberrations

steps.description: The boundary between earlywood and latewood was sharp, and they could be separated with a razor blade under a binocular microscope based on differences in cell colour.
steps.searchable_summary: separate earlywood and latewood fractions from tree rings using razor blade and microscope

steps.description: For obtaining enough wood material to extract purified cellulose, measure isotopic values and minimise the possible influence of outliers caused by potential ecological differences between the individual trees (Borella and Leuenberger, 1998), the earlywood ... fractions sampled trees for each year was carefully pooled prior to cellulose extraction.
steps.searchable_summary: pool earlywood samples from all sampled trees for each year

steps.description: ... and latewood fractions sampled trees for each year was carefully pooled prior to cellulose extraction.
steps.searchable_summary: pool latewood samples from all sampled trees for each year

steps.description: In order to remove the juvenile effect, the inner 20 rings of each core were discarded (Savard et al., 2005).
steps.searchable_summary: discard inner juvenile rings from cores

steps.description: We pooled the annual rings from the samples by mixing all latewood (or earlywood) from the same year and storing them in a microcentrifuge tube (Leavitt, 2010).
steps.searchable_summary: store pooled annual ring samples in microcentrifuge tubes

steps.description: According to the standard procedures of Green (1963) and Loader et al. (1997), ground samples were processed to extract a-cellulose.
steps.searchable_summary: extract alpha-cellulose from ground wood samples using standard procedures (Green 1963, Loader et al. 1997)

steps.description: To obtain better homogenisation of the cellulose, we used an ultrasound machine (JY92-2D; Scientz Industry, Nibgbo, China) to break the cellulose fibres according to the method of Laumer et al. (2009).
steps.searchable_summary: homogenize a-cellulose using ultrasound machine

steps.description: The a-cellulose was then freeze-dried for 72 hours using a vacuum-freeze dryer (Labconco Corporation, Kansas City, MO, USA) prior to the isotope analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: freeze-dry a-cellulose samples for 72 hours

steps.description: About 0.14 to 0.16 mg of a-cellulose of each sample were packed in silver capsules, and then conveyed into a High-Temperature Conversion Element Analyzer (TC/EA) linked to a mass spectrometer (MAT-253, Thermo Electron Corporation, Bremen, Germany) to determine the oxygen isotopic ratios d 18 O/ 16 O) for each earlywood and latewood sample.
steps.searchable_summary: pack a-cellulose samples into silver capsules for isotope analysis

steps.description: ... conveyed into a High-Temperature Conversion Element Analyzer (TC/EA) linked to a mass spectrometer (MAT-253, Thermo Electron Corporation, Bremen, Germany) to determine the oxygen isotopic ratios d 18 O/ 16 O) for each earlywood and latewood sample.
steps.searchable_summary: measure oxygen isotopic ratios (d18O/16O) using TC/EA linked to a mass spectrometer

steps.description: Each sample was analysed four times to obtain a precise result.
steps.searchable_summary: perform four replicate analyses per sample
outputs: multiple raw isotopic ratios per sample

steps.description: The final tree-ring d 18 O values were calculated as the mean from the runs after first excluding outliers.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate mean d18O values from replicate runs, excluding outliers
inputs: multiple raw isotopic ratios per sample

steps.description: We measured the ratio for a benzoic acid working standard with a known d 18 O value (IAEA-601, d 18 O 0 23.3%) every seven measurements to monitor the analytical precision and to calibrate the samples for analytical accuracy (Liu et al., 2009;2012).
steps.searchable_summary: measure standard (IAEA-601) every seven measurements to monitor precision and calibrate accuracy

steps.description: The isotope values were then expressed as the ratio of the value in the sample to that in the cellulose standard IAEA-C 3 (32.2%) to calibrate the treering oxygen measurements.
steps.searchable_summary: calibrate isotope values using standard (IAEA-C3)

steps.description: The replicate runs yielded an SD for each sample of less than 0.3%.
steps.searchable_summary: check that standard deviation of replicate runs is less than 0.3%
inputs: multiple raw isotopic ratios per sample

steps.description: The oxygen isotope ratios were expressed as d 18 O, which represents the per mil deviation relative to the Vienna Standard Mean Ocean Water (VSMOW).
steps.searchable_summary: express d18O values relative to VSMOW standard

methods.description: This method describes the process for collecting tree cores and processing them using standard dendrochronological techniques to create a crossdated tree-ring width chronology.

steps.description: In the present study, we collected cores from dominant fir trees in the temperate forest (2786.1?N, 100813.4?E, ca. 3260 m.a.s.l.) located at Yulong Snowy Mountains, in the northwestern Lijiang in July 2005 ( Fig. 1). We obtained two cores from each of 20 trees, regularly spaced around the circumference at a height of ca. 1.30 m, using 5-mm increment borers (Haglo¨f, Mora, Sweden).
steps.searchable_summary: collect two cores from each of 20 dominant fir trees using increment borers at specified location and height

steps.description: All cores taken from the 20 trees were processed using standard dendrochronological techniques (Stokes and Smiley, 1968);
steps.searchable_summary: process tree cores using standard dendrochronological techniques

steps.description: they were measured at a resolution of 0.01 mm
steps.searchable_summary: measure tree ring widths at 0.01 mm resolution

steps.description: and were crossdated to obtain a reference chronology.
steps.searchable_summary: crossdate tree ring width measurements to align chronologies and obtain a reference chronology

steps.description: For all the samples, cross-correlations were checked with the COFECHA software (Holmes, 1983),
steps.searchable_summary: check cross-correlations using COFECHA software

steps.description: and the ultimate standard chronology for the tree-ring width indices were detrended with a negative exponential
steps.searchable_summary: detrend tree ring width chronology using a negative exponential model

steps.description: and modelled with the autoregressive model by the ARSTAN software (Cook and Holmes, 1986).
steps.searchable_summary: model detrended chronology using autoregressive model with ARSTAN software

steps.description: The final tree-ring width chronology showed good correlations to another chronology that is close to sampling site (see Fig. S1).
steps.searchable_summary: check correlation of final chronology with external chronology
